perturbation.original_text: SUPPLIER shall notify GKT in writing of SUPPLIER’s intent to make any disclosure based on one of the above-listed exceptions at least 30 days in advance (including a description of the informati on to be disclosed and the basis for the claimed exception).
perturbation.changed_text: SUPPLIER shall notify GKT in writing of SUPPLIER’s intent to make any disclosure based on one of the above-listed exceptions at least 7 days in advance (including a description of the informati on to be disclosed and the basis for the claimed exception).
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the notification period for disclosing confidential information from 30 days to 7 days may be insufficient under trade secret laws, which often require reasonable measures to maintain confidentiality, potentially conflicting with the standard.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA) requires reasonable measures to protect trade secrets. Reducing the notification period before disclosing potentially trade-secret information from 30 to 7 days could be deemed unreasonable, especially in complex situations where the disclosing party needs adequate time to assess the situation and respond appropriately.

perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the notification period from 30 days to 7 days, potentially conflicting with the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA), which requires reasonable measures to protect trade secrets. The law explanation states that a shorter notification period could be deemed unreasonable. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 is 'None' and Scraped Law Snippet 2 is a general description of the Uniform Law Commission, the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation both suggest that reducing the notification period could violate the standard of 'reasonable measures' required to protect trade secrets under the UTSA, thus creating a legal contradiction by potentially failing to provide adequate time for the disclosing party to assess and respond appropriately, leading to a non-compliance.

perturbation.original_text: All Confidential Information is and shall remain the property of GK. At any time that GKT may request, SUPPLIER shall return the Confidential Information to GK and shall certify in writing that all copies thereof in its possession have been destroyed.
perturbation.changed_text: All Confidential Information is and shall remain the property of GK. GK may request, at their discretion, that SUPPLIER return the Confidential Information to GK; however, SUPPLIER is not required to certify in writing that all copies thereof in its possession have been destroyed.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the mandatory certification of destruction of confidential information, the clause creates a situation where the supplier may retain copies indefinitely, potentially violating trade secret laws requiring reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 18 U.S. Code § 1832 - Theft of trade secrets
perturbation.law_explanation: 18 U.S.C. § 1832 prohibits the theft of trade secrets. By removing the requirement for the supplier to certify the destruction of confidential information, the contract weakens the protections afforded to trade secrets, as it allows the supplier to retain copies, which could increase the risk of misappropriation or theft. This contradicts the intent of 18 U.S.C. § 1832, which seeks to prevent the unauthorized taking of trade secrets.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 18 U.S. Code § 1832 - Theft of trade secrets | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 18 U.S. Code § 1832 - Theft of trade secrets U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) Whoever, with intent to convert a trade secret , that is related to a product or service used in or intended for use in interstate or foreign commerce, to the economic benefit of anyone other than the owner thereof, and intending or knowing that the offense will, injure any owner of that trade secret , knowingly— (1) steals, or without authorization appropriates, takes, carries away, or conceals, or by fraud, artifice, or deception obtains such information; (2) without authorization copies, duplicates, sketches, draws, photographs, downloads, uploads, alters, destroys, photocopies, replicates, transmits, delivers, sends, mails, communicates, or conveys such information; (3) receives, buys, or possesses such information, knowing the same to have been stolen or appropriated, obtained, or converted without authorization; (4) attempts to commit any offense described in paragraphs (1) through (3); or (5) conspires with one or more other persons to commit any offense described in paragraphs (1) through (3), and one or more of such persons do any act to effect the object of the conspiracy, shall, except as provided in subsection (b), be fined under this title or imprisoned not more than 10 years, or both. (b) Any organization that commits any offense described in subsection (a) shall be fined not more than the greater of $5,000,000 or 3 times the value of the stolen trade secret to the organization, including expenses for research and design and other costs of reproducing the trade secret that the organization has thereby avoided. (Added Pub. L. 104–294, title I, § 101(a) , Oct. 11, 1996 , 110 Stat. 3489 ; amended Pub. L. 112–236, § 2 , Dec. 28, 2012 , 126 Stat. 1627 ; Pub. L. 114–153, § 3(a)(1) , May 11, 2016 , 130 Stat. 382
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the mandatory requirement for the supplier to certify in writing the destruction of confidential information. This directly weakens the protection of trade secrets. According to 18 U.S.C. § 1832, as detailed in both scraped snippets, the law prohibits the theft of trade secrets. By allowing the supplier to retain copies without certification of destruction, the changed contract increases the risk of misappropriation. This directly contradicts the intent of 18 U.S.C. § 1832, which aims to prevent the unauthorized taking of trade secrets. The original text ensured a process to confirm destruction, while the changed text removes this safeguard, leading to a situation where the supplier could retain copies indefinitely, creating a potential violation. Therefore, a contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: The parties hereby agree to submit all disputes hereunder to arbitration in Connecticut, USA in accordance with the rules of the International Chamber of Commerce . All arbitration proceedings shall be conducted exclusively in the English language before a single arbitrator. Notwithstanding the foregoing, if SUPPLIER is alleged to have breached this Agreement, then GK shall be entitled to seek injunctive relief from any court of competent jurisdiction.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties hereby agree to submit all disputes hereunder to arbitration in Connecticut, USA, with no right to appeal the arbitrator's decision. All arbitration proceedings shall be conducted exclusively in the English language before a single arbitrator. Notwithstanding the foregoing, if SUPPLIER is alleged to have breached this Agreement, then GK shall be entitled to seek injunctive relief from any court of competent jurisdiction.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the right to appeal an arbitration decision may violate the FAA, which allows for vacatur of arbitration awards in specific circumstances.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Federal Arbitration Act (FAA) outlines grounds for vacating an arbitration award, such as fraud, evident partiality, or misconduct by the arbitrator. By explicitly stating that there is 'no right to appeal,' the clause attempts to prevent parties from seeking judicial review even when grounds for vacatur under the FAA exist. This directly contradicts the FAA, which provides a limited right to challenge arbitration awards in federal court.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 9 U.S. Code § 10 - Same; vacation; grounds; rehearing | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 9 U.S. Code § 10 - Same; vacation; grounds; rehearing U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) In any of the following cases the United States court in and for the district wherein the award was made may make an order vacating the award upon the application of any party to the arbitration— (1) where the award was procured by corruption, fraud, or undue means; (2) where there was evident partiality or corruption in the arbitrators, or either of them; (3) where the arbitrators were guilty of misconduct in refusing to postpone the hearing, upon sufficient cause shown, or in refusing to hear evidence pertinent and material to the controversy; or of any other misbehavior by which the rights of any party have been prejudiced; or (4) where the arbitrators exceeded their powers, or so imperfectly executed them that a mutual, final, and definite award upon the subject matter submitted was not made. (b) If an award is vacated and the time within which the agreement required the award to be made has not expired, the court may, in its discretion, direct a rehearing by the arbitrators. (c) The United States district court for the district wherein an award was made that was issued pursuant to section 580 of title 5 may make an order vacating the award upon the application of a person, other than a party to the arbitration, who is adversely affected or aggrieved by the award, if the use of arbitration or the award is clearly inconsistent with the factors set forth in section 572 of title 5 . (July 30, 1947, ch. 392, 61 Stat. 672 ; Pub. L. 101–552, § 5 , Nov. 15, 1990 , 104 Stat. 2745 ; Pub. L. 102–354, § 5(b)(4) , Aug. 26, 1992 , 106 Stat. 946 ; Pub. L. 107–169, § 1 , May 7, 2002 , 116 Stat. 132 .) Derivation Act Feb. 12, 1925, ch. 213, § 10, 43 Stat. 885 . Editorial Notes Amendments 2002—Subsec. (a)(1) to
perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly removes the right to appeal the arbitrator's decision. This contradicts the FAA, which provides grounds for vacating an arbitration award under 9 U.S. Code § 10. Specifically, 9 U.S. Code § 10 (a) states that a court may vacate an award if it was procured by corruption, fraud, or undue means; if there was evident partiality or corruption in the arbitrators; if the arbitrators were guilty of misconduct; or if the arbitrators exceeded their powers. By removing the right to appeal, the contract attempts to prevent a party from challenging the award even if these grounds for vacatur exist, which is a direct conflict with the statute.

perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information does not include the following: a) information which was already in the public domain at the time of disclosure; or b) information which, though originally confidential, subsequently becomes part of the public domain through no fault of SUPPLIER; or c) information which was properly in SUPPLIER’s possession prior to receipt thereof from GK, as reasonably evidenced by written records or other writings, or by actual use by SUPPLIER prior to the disclosure by GK.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information does not include the following: a) information which was already in the public domain at the time of disclosure; or b) information which, though originally confidential, subsequently becomes part of the public domain, regardless of SUPPLIER's fault; or c) information which was in SUPPLIER’s possession prior to receipt thereof from GK.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the 'through no fault of SUPPLIER' clause from the public domain exception and the 'reasonably evidenced' clause may be seen as providing inadequate protection for trade secrets under state law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Connecticut Uniform Trade Secrets Act requires the use of reasonable methods to maintain the secrecy of claimed trade secrets. Changing the language regarding exceptions to confidentiality could undermine the 'reasonable efforts' requirement, as it might allow information to lose protection even if the supplier's actions contributed to its becoming public.

(b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who (A) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) at the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his knowledge of the trade secret was (i) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use, including but not limited to disclosures made under section 1-210, sections 31-40j to 31-40p, inclusive, or subsection (c) of section 12-62; or (iii) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (C) before a material change of his position, knew or had reason to know that i
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens trade secret protection, contradicting the intent of the Connecticut Uniform Trade Secrets Act. By removing 'through no fault of SUPPLIER' and 'reasonably evidenced,' the contract allows confidential information to lose protection even if the supplier's actions contributed to its becoming public or if their claim of prior possession lacks reasonable evidence. According to the Law Explanation, this undermines the 'reasonable efforts' requirement, which Connecticut law implies is necessary to maintain trade secret protection. Scraped Law Snippet 2 defines 'Improper means' which includes 'breach or inducement of a breach of duty to maintain secrecy.' Allowing confidential information to lose protection due to the supplier's fault, or without reasonable evidence of prior possession, could be construed as a failure to maintain secrecy, thereby creating a potential breach.

perturbation.original_text: SUPPLIER agrees to hold all Confidential Information in strict confidence and will not disclose or use the Confidential Information for its benefit or the benefit of any other company or entity anywhere in the world or any other purpose other than for the Purpose set forth in the recitals herein.
perturbation.changed_text: SUPPLIER agrees to hold all Confidential Information in confidence and will not disclose the Confidential Information for its direct benefit or the direct benefit of another company. Use of confidential information for other purposes is permitted with GK's verbal consent.
perturbation.explanation: Allowing disclosure with verbal consent contradicts the Statute of Frauds in many states (including Connecticut) which requires that certain agreements, especially those involving significant intellectual property or long-term obligations, be in writing to be enforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: Connecticut's Statute of Frauds requires that certain agreements be in writing to be enforceable. Permitting verbal consent for the use of confidential information contradicts this requirement, as it allows modifications to the confidentiality agreement without a written record, making it difficult to enforce the terms and potentially leading to disputes about the scope of permitted use.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Sec. 52-550. Statute of frauds; written agreement or memorandum. :: Chapter 923 — Statute of Frauds (contains Secs. 52-550 to 52-551) :: Title 52 — Civil Actions (contains Chapters 895 to 929) :: 2005 Connecticut Code :: Connecticut Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of the Connecticut General Statutes 2024 Conn. Gen. Stat. 2023 Conn. Gen. Stat. 2022 Conn. Gen. Stat. 2005 Conn. Gen. Stat. (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2005 Connecticut Code - Sec. 52-550. Statute of frauds; written agreement or memorandum. Sec. 52-550. Statute of frauds; written agreement or memorandum. (a) No civil action may be maintained in the following cases unless the agreement, or a memorandum of the agreement, is made in writing and signed by the party, or the agent of the party, to be charged: (1) Upon any agreement to charge any executor or administrator, upon a special promise to answer damages out of his own property; (2) against any person upon any special promise to answer for the debt, default or miscarriage of another; (3) upon any agreement made upon consideration of marriage; (4) upon any agreement for the sale of real property or any interest in or concerning real property; (5) upon any agreement that is not to be performed within one year from the making thereof; or (6) upon any agreement for a loan in an amount which exceeds fifty thousand dollars. (b) This section shall not apply to parol agreements for hiring or leasing real property, or any interest therein, for one year or less, in pursuance of which the leased premises have been or are actually occupied by the lessee, or any person claiming under him, during any part of the term. (1949 Rev., S. 8293; P.A. 82-160, S. 218; P.A. 89-338, S. 3; May Sp. Sess. P.A. 92-11, S. 39, 70.) History: P.A. 82-160 replaced "estate" with "property", rephrased the section and inserted Subsec. indicators; P.A. 89-338 amended Subsec. (a) by adding Subdiv. (6) re an agreement for a loan in
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a contradiction by allowing verbal consent for using confidential information, whereas the Statute of Frauds, as described in the Perturbation Explanation, Law Explanation, and Snippet 2, requires certain agreements, including modifications to them, to be in writing to be enforceable. Snippet 2, specifically Sec. 52-550 (a), states that 'No civil action may be maintained in the following cases unless the agreement, or a memorandum of the agreement, is made in writing and signed by the party...'. The change permits a modification without a written record, directly conflicting with this requirement.

perturbation.original_text: Information disclosed in other that written form shall be considered Confidential Information only to the extent GK summarizes the same in a written form that is transmitted to SUPPLIER within thirty (30) calendar days of the non-written disclosures.
perturbation.changed_text: Information disclosed in other that written form shall be considered Confidential Information only to the extent GK summarizes the same in a written form that is transmitted to SUPPLIER within ninety (90) calendar days of the non-written disclosures.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the period for summarizing non-written disclosures to 90 days may not provide reasonable protection of the confidential information and it increases the risk of disputes about the scope and nature of the information that has been disclosed. This could violate trade secret laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: Connecticut Uniform Trade Secrets Act requires use of reasonable methods to maintain the secrecy of claimed trade secrets. Increasing the time to summarize to 90 days would be considered unreasonable, as the disclosed information is not protected by written form in this time period.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Sec. 35-51. Gen. Stat. 2023 Conn. Gen. Stat. 2022 Conn. Gen. Stat. 2005 Conn. Gen. Stat. (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2005 Connecticut Code - Sec. 35-51. Definitions. Sec. 35-51. Definitions. As used in this chapter, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means, including searching through trash. (b) "Misappropriation" means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who (A) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) at the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his knowledge of the trade secret was (i) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use, including but not limited to disclosures made under section 1-210, sections 31-40j to 31-40p, inclusive, or subsection (c) of section 12-62; or (iii) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (C) before a material change of his position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (c) "Person" means a natural person, corporation, limited liability com
perturbation.justification: The modified text extends the period for summarizing non-written disclosures from 30 to 90 days. The Law Explanation states that this increased timeframe could be considered unreasonable under the Connecticut Uniform Trade Secrets Act, as the disclosed information is not protected by written form during this extended period. Scraped Law Snippet 2 defines "Misappropriation" as the disclosure or use of a trade secret without consent, implying a need for measures to maintain secrecy. By extending the summarization period, the changed text weakens the protection of confidential information during this period, potentially leading to misappropriation. The Perturbation Explanation also points out that this extension could increase the risk of disputes about the disclosed information, further supporting the existence of a contradiction.
